tasks.title: Warm-up: Create a Replicated Topic and Verify Replication State
tasks.prompt: Set up a 3-broker Docker environment and create an 'orders' topic with replication.factor=3, partitions=3, and min.insync.replicas=2. After creation, run kafka-topics.sh --describe to display the Leader, Replicas, and Isr fields for each partition, and confirm that all three broker IDs appear in the Isr.
tasks.starter:
# 1. docker-compose.yml (minimal 3-broker configuration)
# Save the content below as docker-compose.yml and run 'docker-compose up -d'
#
# version: '3.8'
# services:
#   zookeeper:
#     image: confluentinc/cp-zookeeper:7.6.0
#     environment:
#       ZOOKEEPER_CLIENT_PORT: 2181
#
#   broker1:
#     image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.6.0
#     depends_on: [zookeeper]
#     ports: ['9092:9092']
#     environment:
#       KAFKA_BROKER_ID: 1
#       KAFKA_ZOOKEEPER_CONNECT: zookeeper:2181
#       KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9092
#
#   broker2:
#     image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.6.0
#     depends_on: [zookeeper]
#     ports: ['9093:9093']
#     environment:
#       KAFKA_BROKER_ID: 2
#       KAFKA_ZOOKEEPER_CONNECT: zookeeper:2181
#       KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9093
#
#   broker3:
#     image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.6.0
#     depends_on: [zookeeper]
#     ports: ['9094:9094']
#     environment:
#       KAFKA_BROKER_ID: 3
#       KAFKA_ZOOKEEPER_CONNECT: zookeeper:2181
#       KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9094

# 2. Topic creation command (fill in the blanks)
kafka-topics.sh --create \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions ___ \
  --replication-factor ___ \
  --config min.insync.replicas=___

# 3. State verification command
kafka-topics.sh --describe \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
tasks.solution:
# Topic creation
kafka-topics.sh --create \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions 3 \
  --replication-factor 3 \
  --config min.insync.replicas=2

# State verification
kafka-topics.sh --describe \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

# Expected output (broker IDs may differ by environment)
# Topic: orders   TopicId: ...   PartitionCount: 3   ReplicationFactor: 3   Configs: min.insync.replicas=2
#   Topic: orders  Partition: 0  Leader: 1  Replicas: 1,2,3  Isr: 1,2,3
#   Topic: orders  Partition: 1  Leader: 2  Replicas: 2,3,1  Isr: 2,3,1
#   Topic: orders  Partition: 2  Leader: 3  Replicas: 3,1,2  Isr: 3,1,2
#
# Checkpoints:
#   - ReplicationFactor: 3
#   - Configs: min.insync.replicas=2
#   - All three broker IDs appear in the Isr list for every partition
tasks.explanation: replication.factor=3 instructs Kafka to distribute three copies of each partition across three brokers. The Isr (In-Sync Replicas) is the set of replicas that are sufficiently caught up with the leader — in a healthy cluster, all three broker IDs should be present. min.insync.replicas=2 means that an acks=all write succeeds only when at least two replicas are in the ISR. In the --describe output, pay attention to three distinct fields: Leader (the broker currently handling reads and writes), Replicas (the full list of all replicas), and Isr (the replicas currently in sync). Understanding the difference between these three is the foundation for all subsequent labs.
hints: Set both --partitions and --replication-factor to 3, and don't forget --config min.insync.replicas=2.
If the Isr column shows all three broker IDs, the cluster is healthy. If you see only two or fewer, one of the containers may still be starting up — wait 10–20 seconds and check again.
If you're running commands inside a container (e.g., docker-compose exec broker1 kafka-topics ...), use the service name as the bootstrap server (e.g., broker1:9092) instead of localhost.

tasks.title: Core Lab 1: Compare Producer Behavior Across acks Settings
tasks.prompt: Send messages to the same 'orders' topic using three different producer configurations in turn: acks=0, acks=1, and acks=all. Confirm that each sends successfully. Then, with acks=all still active, stop two brokers (docker stop) so that min.insync.replicas can no longer be satisfied, and deliberately trigger a NotEnoughReplicasException.
tasks.starter:
# --- acks=0 (fire-and-forget) ---
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --producer-property acks=0
# When the prompt appears, type 'msg-acks0' and press Ctrl+C

# --- acks=1 (leader acknowledgment) ---
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --producer-property acks=1
# Type 'msg-acks1' and press Ctrl+C

# --- acks=all (full ISR acknowledgment) ---
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --producer-property acks=all
# Type 'msg-acks-all' and press Ctrl+C

# --- Reproduce insufficient ISR (stop two brokers) ---
# Note the current Isr state before stopping the containers
docker stop <broker2_container_name> <broker3_container_name>

# After stopping the brokers, run the acks=all producer again and observe the error
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --producer-property acks=all
tasks.solution:
# acks=0 and acks=1 both succeed immediately.
# acks=all also succeeds when the ISR has 3 members (>= min.insync.replicas=2)

# Stop two brokers (verify container names with docker ps)
docker stop kafka-intermediate-broker2-1 kafka-intermediate-broker3-1

# Run the acks=all producer after the ISR shrinks to 1
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --producer-property acks=all
# After sending a message, you should see an error like:
# [2024-...] WARN Error while fetching metadata ... NotEnoughReplicasException
# org.apache.kafka.common.errors.NotEnoughReplicasException:
#   Messages are rejected since there are fewer in-sync replicas than required.

# Key observations:
# - acks=0, acks=1: fast response with no replication check (risk of data loss)
# - acks=all: waits for confirmation from all ISR members (safest; rejected when ISR is insufficient)
# - With min.insync.replicas=2 and ISR=1, NotEnoughReplicasException is thrown
tasks.explanation: acks=0 sends the message to the network without waiting for any response, giving the highest throughput at the cost of potential data loss. acks=1 considers the write successful once the leader receives it, but if the leader dies before replicating to followers, the message is lost. acks=all waits for all ISR replicas to acknowledge the write, making it the safest option. When combined with min.insync.replicas=2, if the ISR drops below 2, writes are rejected outright. This is Kafka's durability-first philosophy: if the data cannot be safely replicated, it is better to refuse the write. Once the brokers are restarted and the ISR recovers to 2 or more, writes resume immediately.
hints: Run docker ps first to get the exact container names, then pass them to docker stop.

If the acks=all producer exits silently without showing an error, the error may be written only to stderr. Add --producer-property enable.idempotence=false or redirect stderr with 2>&1, or use a Python/Java client to see the error more clearly.
Run kafka-topics.sh --describe before and after stopping the brokers to visually confirm the Isr column changing from '1,2,3' to '1'. This makes the concept much more concrete.

tasks.title: Core Lab 2: Force a Leader Failure and Observe Leader Election
tasks.prompt: After restoring all three brokers to a healthy state, use --describe to identify the current leader broker for Partition 0. Stop that leader broker with docker stop, then immediately run --describe repeatedly to watch Partition 0's Leader ID change to another broker and the Isr shrink to two. Also verify that producers and consumers continue to operate during the transition.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Restart brokers if they were stopped in the previous lab
docker start <broker2_container> <broker3_container>
# Repeat the command below until the Isr recovers to 3 (may take 10–30 seconds)
kafka-topics.sh --describe --topic orders --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

# 2. Note the current leader for Partition 0
# Example output: Partition: 0  Leader: 1  Replicas: 1,2,3  Isr: 1,2,3
# → Leader=1 means broker1 is the leader for Partition 0

# 3. Terminal A: keep a consumer running
kafka-console-consumer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --from-beginning

# 4. Terminal B: keep a producer running (acks=all)
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic orders \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --producer-property acks=all

# 5. Terminal C: stop the leader broker, then immediately observe state
docker stop <leader_broker_container>   # the container matching the Leader ID
kafka-topics.sh --describe --topic orders --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
# Run the above command 2–3 times at 5-second intervals and record the Leader value each time
tasks.solution:
# Full example flow (assuming broker1 with Leader=1 is stopped)

# Before stopping
# Partition: 0  Leader: 1  Replicas: 1,2,3  Isr: 1,2,3

# docker stop kafka-intermediate-broker1-1

# Within a few seconds of stopping
# Partition: 0  Leader: 2  Replicas: 1,2,3  Isr: 2,3
#   → Leader switched from 1 to 2
#   → broker 1 removed from Isr, now 2 members
#   → Isr 2 >= min.insync.replicas 2, so reads and writes continue normally

# Producer/consumer behavior:
# - Terminal B: after a brief reconnection attempt, messages resume sending normally
# - Terminal A: consumer continues receiving messages without interruption

# Restart broker1
docker start kafka-intermediate-broker1-1

# After 30–60 seconds, verify that broker 1 rejoins the Isr
# Partition: 0  Leader: 2  Replicas: 1,2,3  Isr: 2,3,1
# (The leader stays at 2; preferred replica rebalancing can be triggered manually or automatically)
tasks.explanation: When the leader goes down, the Kafka controller (the leader controller in KRaft mode) immediately elects a new leader from the remaining ISR members. Because every ISR replica is guaranteed to hold all committed data, the transition happens with no data loss. Producers and consumers update their metadata and reconnect to the new leader, so there may be a brief reconnection delay but no message loss. Once the failed broker is restarted, it catches up with the new leader by replicating any missing data, then rejoins the ISR. This ISR-based recovery mechanism is the core of Kafka's data-loss-free fault tolerance.
hints: You need to run --describe quickly after docker stop to catch the leader transition in progress. Using 'watch -n1 kafka-topics.sh --describe ...' in a shell loop makes it easy to observe continuously.

Seeing a brief warning like 'WARN [Consumer ...] Connection to node 1 could not be established.' in the consumer terminal, followed by resumed message delivery, is normal and expected — it does not indicate a fatal error.
The broker you stop may not be the leader for Partition 0. Always check the Leader column for 'Partition: 0' in the --describe output and stop exactly that broker.

tasks.title: Advanced: Reproduce the replication.factor / min.insync.replicas Trap and Compare the Golden Combination
tasks.prompt:
Create two topics with the configurations below, then compare their write behavior when one broker is stopped.

- Topic A: replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=3, acks=all  (the trap)
- Topic B: replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=2, acks=all  (the golden combination)

Send acks=all messages to both topics before and after stopping one broker. Record and compare the results.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create both topics
kafka-topics.sh --create \
  --topic trap-topic \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions 1 \
  --replication-factor 3 \
  --config min.insync.replicas=3

kafka-topics.sh --create \
  --topic golden-topic \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions 1 \
  --replication-factor 3 \
  --config min.insync.replicas=2

# 2. Before stopping a broker — confirm both topics accept messages
# (run each command, type one message, press Ctrl+C)
kafka-console-producer.sh --topic trap-topic   --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --producer-property acks=all
kafka-console-producer.sh --topic golden-topic --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --producer-property acks=all

# 3. Stop one broker
docker stop <any_one_broker>

# 4. After stopping — attempt to send acks=all messages to both topics
# Record the result for each:
# trap-topic   result: ___
# golden-topic result: ___
tasks.solution:
# Results summary

# After stopping one broker, trap-topic (min.insync.replicas=3) → ISR drops to 2
# → ISR(2) < min.insync.replicas(3) → NotEnoughReplicasException
# → A single broker failure completely halts writes (the trap)

# After stopping one broker, golden-topic (min.insync.replicas=2) → ISR drops to 2
# → ISR(2) >= min.insync.replicas(2) → writes continue normally
# → Tolerates 1 failure while maintaining durability (the golden combination)

# Comparison table
# +-----------------+--------------------+------------------+------------------+
# | Setting         | Healthy (ISR=3)    | 1 failure (ISR=2)| 2 failures (ISR=1)|
# +-----------------+--------------------+------------------+------------------+
# | min.insync=3    | Writes OK          | Writes rejected  | Writes rejected  |
# | min.insync=2    | Writes OK          | Writes OK        | Writes rejected  |
# +-----------------+--------------------+------------------+------------------+

# Conclusion: setting replication.factor and min.insync.replicas to the same value
#             halts all writes on a single broker failure.
#             Always set min.insync.replicas to (replication.factor - 1).
tasks.explanation: replication.factor=3 with min.insync.replicas=3 means 'all three replicas must be alive for writes to be permitted.' Even a single broker failure drops the ISR to 2, which is less than min.insync.replicas=3, and writes stop immediately. This is a classic example of an excessive durability requirement sacrificing availability. By contrast, min.insync.replicas=2 (replication.factor - 1) tolerates one broker failure while still writing to at least two copies, striking the right balance between durability and availability. This 'golden combination' is the most widely recommended Kafka configuration in production. Only when two brokers fail simultaneously will writes be rejected, preventing data loss.
hints: The NotEnoughReplicasException may only appear in stderr and not be visible in the console producer output. Redirect stderr using 2>&1, or verify the error indirectly by confirming that the Isr count has dropped below min.insync.replicas.

To compare both topics clearly, run them simultaneously in different terminals: Terminal A for the trap-topic producer, Terminal B for the golden-topic producer, and Terminal C to handle the broker stop.
After finishing this lab, run docker-compose down -v to clean up the environment, then docker-compose up -d for a fresh start before the next lab — this avoids any configuration conflicts.
